triple.what: Started working at age 5 for my mom and dad at Casa Ricardo's Photography. My father's photographs were seen by millions — and nobody ever read the name on the back.
triple.proud: Before I wrote a line of code, I learned the maker's name doesn't have to be on the work for the work to matter. Forty years later, I'm finally putting mine on the front.

triple.what: At 15 I built my father his first computer network — COAX, MS-DOS — then upgraded it to Windows 3.11 for Workgroups and wrote an Access database that ran the entire company.
triple.proud: Fifteen years old and a real business was already running on software I built. The training started early.

triple.what: Champion-level athlete across multiple sports — medals and captain badges on the letterman's jacket, with more than one trip to CIF.
triple.proud: I learned to perform when it counts and lead a team there. The instinct that wins seasons is the one that ships software on deadline.

triple.what: Music major at Fullerton — and a singer and songwriter. I chose music because I already had more software and production hours than the professors did.
triple.proud: Rigor and taste in the same hands. The person who certifies hardware to a giant's spec also writes songs.

triple.what: In 1997/98 at Custom Business Solutions I made the argument that defined the company: we had to own our code — stop depending on PosiTouch and RDC, and build our own.
triple.proud: I bet the company on owning our work instead of renting someone else's. It took years to prove me right. It did.

triple.what: At Javelin Systems (public, JVLN, under Norman Campbell) I spent a full year getting the Javelin Wedge — 'The McWedge' — certified by McDonald's Corp. In support, nobody got past me.
triple.proud: When it's that big, you don't get to be almost right. Hardware down to the metal — it's why I can drop to pure C on any stack today.

triple.what: At Koo Koo Roo I built software on the Javelin Wedge ('Clucky') and designed a plug-and-play rollout — open the box, tap once, and it reconfigures and reboots itself into a working terminal. Color-coded cables, printed manuals.
triple.proud: Open the box, plug it in, tap once — it just works. We cut the rollout cost in half by doing it right.

triple.what: I wrote the enterprise software layer that made PosiTouch sellable to big chains — reports, change management, integrations, large-data access, mining, data cubes, alerts. That layer is what closed CBS's major deals.
triple.proud: PosiTouch had no enterprise. I built it all. That's the software that won the rooms.

triple.what: For the first build of NorthStar I took my dev team and went aboard a Norwegian Cruise Line ship to finish it live. We got them happy 20 minutes before they'd have thrown us off for Micros.
triple.proud: The fastest factory on earth is a restaurant — and the highest stakes is a ship at sea with no exit. We shipped with 20 minutes to spare.

triple.what: I co-led the Stacked: Food Well Built build with Brian Pearson — the iPad-pioneer restaurant in Torrance/Del Amo. We started on Android two months before the iPad was announced, then pivoted to iPad the day Jobs unveiled it.
triple.proud: Two months in on Android, Jobs announced the iPad, and we pivoted that day. That call is why Stacked made the news.

triple.what: Rick Smith handed me a prototype at the Cheesecake Factory. In two weeks I turned it into the NorthStar Recipe Viewer — a product CBS still sells today, used by Golden Corral across 500 locations.
triple.proud: Code I wrote in two weeks is still a shipping product years later. That's what built-right looks like.

triple.what: I wrote the entire back-office reporting suite the Cheesecake Factory ran on for 18 years. Across every deal I was lead architect and product owner — I hired, I fired, I owned the budget, I set the vision, and I answered for it.
triple.proud: I owned the whole thing — vision, budget, team, and the code that can't be wrong. I hire, I ship, I close, I answer for it.

triple.what: To scale the company, I moved to the Philippines and stood up offshore development — building a second engineering org with CI/CD, DevOps, and Scrum, and fighting to send my developers to conferences.
triple.proud: I didn't just write the software. I built the company that builds it, across an ocean — and I invested in my people's craft.

triple.what: After 40 years building systems that ran under other companies' names, I'm putting my name on my own work. joeycastillo.us — everything I publish ships under one signature.
triple.proud: Forty years of code under other people's names. Now everything ships under mine. The name is the warranty.
